perturbation.original_text: The term “ Confidential Information ” means any and all information that is or has been received by either Party (the “ Recipient ”) from the other Party (the “ Disclosing Party ”), or which the Recipient has had access to, during the four (4)-month period commencing on the Effective Date (the “ Term ”), and that is either (a) marked as “confidential,” “proprietary,” or such similar term, or (b) non-public information related to the Disclosing Party’s business, which the Receiving Party should reasonably know is confidential based on the nature of such information.
perturbation.changed_text: The term “ Confidential Information ” means any and all information that is or has been received by either Party (the “ Recipient ”) from the other Party (the “ Disclosing Party ”), or which the Recipient has had access to, during the four (4)-month period commencing on the Effective Date (the “ Term ”), and that is either (a) marked as “confidential,” “proprietary,” or such similar term, or (b) non-public information related to the Disclosing Party’s business, irrespective of whether the Receiving Party should reasonably know is confidential based on the nature of such information.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the definition of Confidential Information to include all non-public information related to the Disclosing Party's business, regardless of whether the Receiving Party should reasonably know it's confidential, it introduces a contradiction. This perturbation alters the criteria for what constitutes Confidential Information, making it more expansive and removes the 'reasonableness' assessment, thus, it's no longer necessary for the Receiving Party to reasonably know the information is confidential. This directly impacts Section 3, which outlines the obligations of non-disclosure and confidentiality based on this definition, creating uncertainty about what information is truly protected.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Non-Disclosure and Confidentiality . The Recipient shall not (a) use the Confidential Information except for the Business Purpose, or (b) disclose or make the Confidential Information available to any person or entity (other than Related Parties, as defined below) without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party. Each Party shall take reasonable security precautions to protect the Confidential Information, at least as strict as the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential and proprietary information of a similar nature. Without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient of Confidential Information shall restrict the disclosure and availability of Confidential Information to Recipient’s financial, tax, or legal advisors, or to its directors, officers, or employees with a demonstrable need to know such Confidential Information (each, a “ Related Party ”, and, collectively, the “ Related Parties ”), provided that any such Related Party either agrees to be bound by this Agreement or is already bound by confidentiality obligations no less restrictive than those in this Agreement. Each Party shall be responsible for the breach of this Agreement by any Related Party. Neither Party shall use any Confidential Information received by it to develop a product or service which competes with or imitates products of the Disclosing Party or engage in reverse engineering to develop similar products or services using the Confidential Information (it being acknowledged and understood that both Parties are engaged in the business of beer brewing and that competing beer products are regularly independently developed within the beer industry using the same or similar raw materials, processes and packaging). The Recipient shall prevent commingling of the Confidential Information with similar information or material of other third parties. The obligations of confidentiality and protection under this Agreement shall survive for three (3) years following the expiration of the Term.
perturbation.justification: The original text defined 'Confidential Information' with a 'reasonableness' clause (the Receiving Party should reasonably know it's confidential). The changed text removes this clause, making the definition broader. Section 3, the contradicted text, outlines confidentiality obligations based on the original definition of Confidential Information. Because the definition is changed to be much broader, the burden and scope of Section 3's requirements dramatically increases. This creates a direct contradiction because the Receiving Party's obligations are now tied to information they might not reasonably know is confidential.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall not include any information that: (a) prior to its disclosure by the Disclosing Party is already lawfully and rightfully known by or available to the Recipient as evidenced by prior written records; (b) through no wrongful act, fault or negligence on the part of the Recipient is or hereafter becomes part of the public domain; (c) is lawfully received by the Recipient from a third party without restriction and without breach of this agreement or any other agreement; (d) is approved for public release or use by written authorization of the Disclosing Party; (e) the Recipient can demonstrate was independently developed by it without reference to the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information; or (f) is disclosed pursuant to the requirement or request of a governmental agency or court of competent jurisdiction to the extent such disclosure is required by a valid law, regulation or court order and sufficient notice is given by the Recipient to the Disclosing Party of any such requirement or request in order to permit the Disclosing Party to seek an appropriate protective order or exemption from such requirement or request.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall not include any information that: (a) prior to its disclosure by the Disclosing Party is already lawfully and rightfully known by or available to the Recipient without the need to be evidenced by prior written records; (b) through no wrongful act, fault or negligence on the part of the Recipient is or hereafter becomes part of the public domain; (c) is lawfully received by the Recipient from a third party without restriction and without breach of this agreement or any other agreement; (d) is approved for public release or use by written authorization of the Disclosing Party; (e) the Recipient can demonstrate was independently developed by it without reference to the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information; or (f) is disclosed pursuant to the requirement or request of a governmental agency or court of competent jurisdiction to the extent such disclosure is required by a valid law, regulation or court order and sufficient notice is given by the Recipient to the Disclosing Party of any such requirement or request in order to permit the Disclosing Party to seek an appropriate protective order or exemption from such requirement or request.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement that prior knowledge be 'evidenced by prior written records', this change contradicts the purpose of Section 6, which requires the Recipient to return or destroy all tangible or electronic forms of Confidential Information, and provide a written certification of such actions. If prior knowledge doesn't need written evidence, it becomes challenging to prove that the Recipient had prior knowledge, thus creating an in-text contradiction, and making it hard to enforce the 'Return of Confidential Information' clause, because it's harder to demonstrate what exactly should be returned or destroyed.
perturbation.contradicted_text: No License or Obligations; Return of Confidential Information. This Agreement does not represent nor imply any agreement or commitment to enter into any further business relationship. This Agreement does not create any agency or partnership relationship between the Parties or authorize a Party to use the other Party’s name or trademarks. As between the Parties, all Confidential Information received by a Recipient shall remain the sole and exclusive property of the Disclosing Party. Upon written request by the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall return to the Disclosing Party, or shall destroy in a manner satisfactory to the Disclosing Party, all tangible or electronic forms of Confidential Information, including any and all copies of the Confidential Information or notes containing the Confidential Information, and shall provide a written certification to the Disclosing Party that all tangible forms of the Confidential Information have been returned or destroyed.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of 'Confidential Information' by removing the requirement for 'prior written records' as evidence of prior knowledge. This creates a contradiction with the 'Return of Confidential Information' clause in the 'Contradicted Text'. Without the 'prior written records', it becomes difficult to enforce the return or destruction of all forms of Confidential Information, because it's harder to demonstrate what should be returned, as per the provided Perturbation Explanation and the definition of Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall not (a) use the Confidential Information except for the Business Purpose, or (b) disclose or make the Confidential Information available to any person or entity (other than Related Parties, as defined below) without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall not (a) use the Confidential Information except for the Business Purpose, or (b) disclose or make the Confidential Information available to any person or entity (other than Related Parties, as defined below) without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, unless compelled by a court order.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'unless compelled by a court order,' there's a potential conflict with Section 2(f) which states that Confidential Information shall not include any information that '(f) is disclosed pursuant to the requirement or request of a governmental agency or court of competent jurisdiction to the extent such disclosure is required by a valid law, regulation or court order and sufficient notice is given by the Recipient to the Disclosing Party of any such requirement or request in order to permit the Disclosing Party to seek an appropriate protective order or exemption from such requirement or request.' Now, Section 3 introduces an exception to non-disclosure for court orders, while Section 2(f) defines information disclosed under court orders as not being Confidential Information in the first place. This raises a conflict about whether information disclosed under a court order remains Confidential Information subject to the other provisions of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Confidential Information shall not include any information that: (a) prior to its disclosure by the Disclosing Party is already lawfully and rightfully known by or available to the Recipient as evidenced by prior written records; (b) through no wrongful act, fault or negligence on the part of the Recipient is or hereafter becomes part of the public domain; (c) is lawfully received by the Recipient from a third party without restriction and without breach of this agreement or any other agreement; (d) is approved for public release or use by written authorization of the Disclosing Party; (e) the Recipient can demonstrate was independently developed by it without reference to the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information; or (f) is disclosed pursuant to the requirement or request of a governmental agency or court of competent jurisdiction to the extent such disclosure is required by a valid law, regulation or court order and sufficient notice is given by the Recipient to the Disclosing Party of any such requirement or request in order to permit the Disclosing Party to seek an appropriate protective order or exemption from such requirement or request.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces 'unless compelled by a court order' as an exception to the non-disclosure obligation in Section 3. However, Section 2(f) of the contract already stipulates that information disclosed pursuant to a court order is not considered Confidential Information. This creates a direct contradiction: Section 3 implies court-ordered disclosure is a permissible exception to the confidentiality of information, while Section 2(f) suggests such information is not confidential at all, leading to a direct conflict regarding whether the disclosed information is subject to the other provisions in the contract regarding confidential information, per the perturbation explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall take reasonable security precautions to protect the Confidential Information, at least as strict as the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential and proprietary information of a similar nature.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party shall take security precautions to protect the Confidential Information as they deem appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'reasonable' and 'at least as strict' criteria, the modified clause conflicts with Section 7, which outlines the remedies for breach of the agreement. If a party takes minimal precautions and a breach occurs, it becomes unclear whether they can be held liable, as the standard for 'security precautions' is no longer clearly defined, creating in-text contradiction. This change lowers the standard of care required and contradicts the provision that seeks to provide remedies for breaching the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Remedies for Breach. The Parties agree that money damages would be inadequate to remedy any breach of this Agreement. As a result, a non-breaching party shall be entitled to seek, and a court of competent jurisdiction may grant, specific performance and 2 injunctive or other equitable relief as a remedy for any breach of this Agreement. Such remedy shall be in addition to all other remedies, including money damages, available to a non-breaching party at law or in equity.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the 'reasonable' and 'at least as strict' criteria for security precautions, directly conflicting with the 'Remedies for Breach' section. The original text sets a clear standard of care, while the changed text allows for arbitrary security measures. This creates uncertainty about liability and contradicts the purpose of the 'Remedies' section, which intends to provide recourse for breaches. The lack of a defined standard weakens the enforceability of the agreement and makes it unclear if minimal precautions would be sufficient, thus creating a direct conflict.

perturbation.original_text: Other than through general employment listings and advertising or through the efforts of employment search firms, each Party shall not, during the Term of this Agreement and for one (1) year thereafter, directly or indirectly, solicit or hire any employee or independent contractor of the other Party, or induce any such person to abandon their relationship with the other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party shall not, during the Term of this Agreement and for one (1) year thereafter, directly solicit any employee or independent contractor of the other Party, or induce any such person to abandon their relationship with the other Party. General employment listings, advertising, and the efforts of employment search firms are permitted.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause stating that it's OK to solicit through 'general employment listings and advertising or through the efforts of employment search firms' the changed text creates a conflict with the original intent of allowing such general solicitations. This contradicts the idea of allowing open recruitment methods, creating an in-text contradiction, and potentially making it harder for either party to hire, especially if they're in the same industry, which the document acknowledges.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Non-Solicitation. Other than through general employment listings and advertising or through the efforts of employment search firms, each Party shall not, during the Term of this Agreement and for one (1) year thereafter, directly or indirectly, solicit or hire any employee or independent contractor of the other Party, or induce any such person to abandon their relationship with the other Party.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly permits solicitation through general employment listings, advertising, and employment search firms. The changed text removes the explicit permission for these methods while stating 'General employment listings, advertising, and the efforts of employment search firms are permitted.' as a separate sentence. The removal of that permission from the main non-solicitation clause contradicts the original intent and creates ambiguity around whether such actions are considered a violation of the non-solicitation agreement. The contradicted text also includes the same initial clause, indicating a direct conflict due to the change.

perturbation.original_text: Upon written request by the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall return to the Disclosing Party, or shall destroy in a manner satisfactory to the Disclosing Party, all tangible or electronic forms of Confidential Information, including any and all copies of the Confidential Information or notes containing the Confidential Information, and shall provide a written certification to the Disclosing Party that all tangible forms of the Confidential Information have been returned or destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon written request by the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall return to the Disclosing Party all tangible forms of Confidential Information. Electronic forms and copies do not need to be returned or destroyed.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that 'Electronic forms and copies do not need to be returned or destroyed,' it directly contradicts the previous requirement to return or destroy 'all tangible or electronic forms of Confidential Information, including any and all copies'. This creates a conflict about what exactly the Recipient is obligated to do with the Confidential Information, creating an in-text contradiction. This creates an issue with what remains the exclusive property of the disclosing party and under what conditions
perturbation.contradicted_text: No License or Obligations; Return of Confidential Information. This Agreement does not represent nor imply any agreement or commitment to enter into any further business relationship. This Agreement does not create any agency or partnership relationship between the Parties or authorize a Party to use the other Party’s name or trademarks. As between the Parties, all Confidential Information received by a Recipient shall remain the sole and exclusive property of the Disclosing Party. Upon written request by the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall return to the Disclosing Party, or shall destroy in a manner satisfactory to the Disclosing Party, all tangible or electronic forms of Confidential Information, including any and all copies of the Confidential Information or notes containing the Confidential Information, and shall provide a written certification to the Disclosing Party that all tangible forms of the Confidential Information have been returned or destroyed.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original requirement for the Recipient to return or destroy 'all tangible or electronic forms of Confidential Information, including any and all copies' to only require the return of 'all tangible forms of Confidential Information' and explicitly states 'Electronic forms and copies do not need to be returned or destroyed.' This directly contradicts the contradicted text that states, 'Upon written request by the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall return to the Disclosing Party, or shall destroy in a manner satisfactory to the Disclosing Party, all tangible or electronic forms of Confidential Information, including any and all copies of the Confidential Information or notes containing the Confidential Information, and shall provide a written certification to the Disclosing Party that all tangible forms of the Confidential Information have been returned or destroyed.' The change introduces a clear inconsistency regarding the handling of electronic forms of confidential information.
